turns.content: What's the difference between using a channel as an event bus versus wrapping it with Arc and Mutex for shared state? I'm trying to figure out why my CI runner is leaking memory.

The core distinction lies in how ownership propagates through your threads. A plain channel like std::sync::mpsc::Sender naturally enforces a single-producer model, which fits most event bus patterns where only the pipeline orchestrator emits events. If you wrap that sender with Arc<Mutex<>> or similar shared state types, you're introducing contention points that can block execution and increase memory pressure due to guard ownership.
I see, but I'm still hitting retain cycles because my test suite spawns many worker threads holding strong references to the event emitter. How do I break that without changing my data structures?

You need to introduce weak references into your graph of connections. Using std::sync::Weak is the standard way here, letting child workers hold Weak pointers while the parent holds Strong ones. This ensures the parent can still send events, but if a worker thread drops its Strong reference, the emitter becomes unreachable and gets collected automatically.
Okay, so I should use Weak for my subscribers and keep Strong only in the orchestrator? But what about broadcasting to multiple different threads without them all holding strong references?

Exactly, you want a broadcast pattern where the parent maintains Strong ownership of the channel while children hold Weak references. When a child needs to receive events, it uses tokio::sync::mpsc or std::sync::mpsc with a separate receiver per thread, but avoids holding strong references back to the sender itself.
Wait, if I'm using multiple receivers and weak links, won't that fragment my memory usage across threads? Is there a way to keep this simpler without managing so many handles explicitly?

You can simplify by having a single central channel managed by the orchestrator thread, and having all worker threads subscribe via Weak references to that same channel. This avoids creating new channels per thread and keeps memory consistent.
That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how exactly weak references prevent cycles in my actual code structure right now? Can you walk me through a concrete example with the types involved?

Sure, imagine your event emitter is an Arc<struct EventEmitter> that contains a Sender. Each worker thread holds a Weak<EventEmitter>. When the thread drops its Strong reference to the emitter, the weak link remains valid until no strong references exist anywhere in the system, at which point the emitter gets collected and the channel closes automatically.

Got it, so I should replace my current Arc<Mutex<>> setup with an Arc<Sender> plus Weak pointers everywhere else? And avoid using Mutex on the channels themselves since that defeats the purpose of async?

Yes, exactly. Use Arc for shared ownership but avoid Mutex around the channel itself unless you're doing something unusual like parallel sending from multiple threads in your orchestrator. For most event bus patterns, a single Sender with Weak references everywhere else is sufficient and much simpler.

Thanks! I'll refactor my CI runner now using weak references instead of Arc<Mutex>. One last thing though, what happens if the parent process dies unexpectedly? Will the workers still get notified somehow?

If the parent dies, the channel closes and all active receivers will eventually block or return an error when trying to receive. Workers holding Weak links won't crash immediately but will detect that no new events are coming once the sender is dropped.
I'm trying to build a simple event system in Rust using channels, but I can't figure out how to send an event to multiple subscribers without creating retain cycles.

That's a classic gotcha when building observer patterns with channels. You should use broadcast channels for the one-to-many pattern and store subscriber handles in a map where the values are weak references so they don't hold onto the sender. Specifically, you can use std::sync::mpsc::broadcast for broadcasting events to multiple receivers and then wrap your subscriber registration logic around std::rc::Weak or std::cell::RefCell with internal mutability if needed.

Okay, but I'm still getting a retain cycle because my Event struct is holding the channel sender, and when I try to send it through a Weak reference in my subscription list, the compiler complains that I can't move out of an immutable context.

The issue is that you're trying to store a shared ownership of the Sender inside your subscriber state without properly handling the borrow checker. You should separate the event data from the channel itself by using std::sync::Arc for your Event struct and then only hold the weak reference to the broadcast sender in your subscription registry. This way, the Arc prevents the cycle while the Weak allows safe dereferencing when needed.
I see, so I need to wrap the event data in an Arc but keep the channel separate? And what exactly should my subscriber registry look like if I'm using weak references?

Exactly. Your subscriber registry should be a HashMap where keys are unique identifiers and values hold weak references to your subscribers or a collection of them via a RefCell with internal mutability so you can add/remove dynamically without breaking ownership rules.

Imagine I have a game engine where the UI thread needs to notify many subsystems whenever a player character spawns. If I just store pointers in an array, the UI might accidentally keep those references alive and prevent the spawned entities from being garbage collected when they fall off-screen.

That's exactly the classic retain cycle problem in Rust. The solution is to use channels as your event bus, specifically std::sync::mpsc for one-to-many broadcasting or tokio::sync::broadcast if you're using async runtime. You'd send SpawnEvent messages through the channel so subscribers react without holding direct references.

Right, but what about when a subscriber needs to observe state changes that happen in other threads? I want a pattern where the observer doesn't need to move or take ownership of data it only reads from.

You're describing the Observer pattern implemented with weak references. Instead of strong pointers, use Arc<Weak<T>> so subscribers can observe changes without creating cycles. For a game engine this is often done via std::sync::Arc combined with Weak to allow safe broadcast updates.
So I'm thinking of using tokio::sync::broadcast and Arc<Weak<T>> together? But what if the subscriber thread panics or gets dropped unexpectedly?

That's a great catch. If you use tokio::sync::broadcast correctly, it handles receiver drops gracefully. The key is ensuring your SpawnEvent messages are sent with proper synchronization and that weak references can be upgraded safely before being used in the subscriber logic.
